definition: Thinking that takes up a theme or directs the mind toward an object, especially in ethical reflection and first-jhāna formulae.
notes: The project keeps thinking as the default because it stays readable and refuses to mystify a term that often names ordinary but disciplined mental activity. Directed thought remains available when the directional aspect is crucial, especially in technical prose. Applied attention can help when the function is closer to setting the mind on a theme, but it should not replace the default in ordinary translation. The entry should be read together with vicāra so that first-jhāna language is not collapsed into either mere discursiveness or later scholastic abstraction. Translators should keep the first-jhāna pair stable as thinking and pondering unless a passage is explicitly being glossed in explanatory prose.

context_rules.context: In most jhāna descriptions and practical doctrinal prose
In technical explanation emphasizing the directional setting of the mind on an object
context_rules.notes: Use when plain thinking sounds too loose.

context_rules.context: In prose emphasizing the function of taking up a meditation theme
context_rules.notes: Allowed explanatory alternate, not the default rendering.

context_rules.context: In ethical contexts such as kusala-vitakka and akusala-vitakka
context_rules.notes: Keep the everyday practical force explicit.

example_phrases.notes: Ethical usage that keeps the term grounded.
Wholesome counterpart in the same ethical family.

version_notes: Expanded to govern both jhāna-factor and ethical-thinking usage and to prevent drift into over-technical scholastic renderings.
